title: Revealing the origin of XMCD in an altermagnet via three-dimensional control of spins
authors: Daire Mallon, Zixuan Wu, Jheng-Cyuan Lin, Ruiwen Xie, Bo Zhao, Charles Godfrey, Qing He, Lucia Iglesias, Pierluigi Gargiani, Manuel Valvidares, Peter Bencok, Francesco Maccherozzi, Larissa S. I. Veiga, Paul Steadman, Manuel Bibes, Hongbin Zhang, Paolo G. Radaelli, Hariom Jani
abstract: Altermagnets are an emerging class of collinear antiferromagnets that exhibit unconventional spin-polarised electronic bands, potentially unlocking new functionalities that do not rely on spin-orbit coupling (SOC). Experimental signatures traditionally associated with spin polarisation, like X-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD), are thus being used as a validation of altermagnetism. However, unlike altermagnetic spin-splitting, these responses require SOC and are not invariant under spin-space rotations. This brings into question the extent to which they can be considered direct signatures of altermagnetism. Here, we exploit the g-wave altermagnet $α$-Fe$_{2}$O$_{3}$ to demonstrate that XMCD is governed precisely by the spin-direction-induced symmetry breaking that altermagnetic spin groups are designed to ignore. Strikingly, the XMCD is highly anisotropic and is decoupled from the weak magnetic canting. We show that this anomalous XMCD can be described by on-site Faraday tensors capturing the locally uncompensated spin-orbital anisotropies - a scenario that can be applied to other altermagnets. Leveraging this, we reconstruct complete vectorial maps of nanoscale textures in $α$-Fe$_{2}$O$_{3}$ thin films, including domain walls and topological solitons, which are promising for building future spintronics and magnonics devices.
summary: This paper reveals that XMCD in altermagnets like hematite is driven by local spin-orbit coupling and anisotropy rather than being a direct signature of altermagnetic spin-splitting. By decoupling the XMCD signal from weak magnetic canting, the authors provide a new method for high-resolution, sign-sensitive imaging of complex magnetic textures.
main_problem: The study investigates whether X-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD) is a direct signature of altermagnetism or a non-co-rotating property driven by spin-orbit coupling and local anisotropy.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that XMCD is not a direct proxy for altermagnetism but arises from spin-direction-induced symmetry breaking and local anisotropy. They successfully used XMCD to resolve the 180-degree ambiguity in the Néel vector orientation during imaging.
method: The study combines X-ray spectroscopy (XMCD/XMLD) and X-ray photoemission electron microscopy (X-PEEM) with DFT and multiplet ligand-field theory calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is alpha-Fe2O3 (hematite), a g-wave altermagnet with a corundum crystal structure. The model utilizes a 3D Faraday tensor framework and magnetic point groups to describe the magneto-optical response.
key_observables: XMCD intensity, XMLD contrast, Néel vector (L), weak magnetization (M), and the effective magnetic dipole vector (T).
important_parameters: Morin transition temperature (Tm), spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, and the azimuthal angle (phi) between the Néel vector and the crystal axes.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the XMCD response can be expanded via 1st and 3rd order Faraday tensors and relies on the accuracy of the DFT+MLFT for electronic structure.
figures_summary: Figures show the evolution of XMCD spectra with spin rotation, the control of the XMCD vector via geometry, and reconstructed 6-color vector maps of the Néel vector orientation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of XMCD as a proxy for altermagnetism, presents experimental measurements of spin rotation and anisotropy, provides a theoretical framework using Faraday tensors and symmetry groups, and concludes with the application of these findings to nanoscale texture imaging.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 | The corundum crystal structure of α-Fe2O3 and the accessible magnetic point groups. The MPGs include 2’/𝑚’ (spins along the 2-fold axis), 2/𝑚 (spins orthogonal to the 2-fold axis), 1ത (spins in a low symmetry direction) and 3ത𝑚 (spins along the high-symmetry 3-fold axis). The Fe-spins are visualised within the adjacent face-sharing FeO6 octahedra, showing the opposite directions of the magnetic sub-lattices 𝑴ଵ and 𝑴ଶ. The 𝑎-axis and 𝑚-axis are orthogonal and span the basal plane.

Figure 2 | Fe L-edge XMCD spectra with X-ray 𝒌-vector along the 𝒄-axis. (a-d) Experimental geometries (normal incidence) and 𝑳 orientations (defined by antiparallel sublattices - blue arrows) of MPGs present within α-Fe2O3, (a) 2’/𝑚’, (b) 1ത, (c) 2/𝑚 and (d) 3ത𝑚 with respect to crystal axes. (e-h) Measured and (i-l) calculated XMCD spectra for four accessible MPGs. Spectra in (e-g) were measured in the total electron yield mode at T = 300 K. An orthogonal magnetic field was applied to align 𝑳 parallel/antiparallel to the crystal directions indicated (blue/red). The out-of-plane 𝑳 orientation in (h) was achieved by cooling below 𝑇ெ (T = 12 K). (m) XMCD intensity at 𝐿ଶ-edge peak (722.25 eV)...

Figure 3 | Fe L-edge XMCD spectra with X-ray 𝒌-vector oblique and orthogonal to the 𝒄-axis. (a-c) Experimental geometries (oblique and normal incidence) and 𝑳 orientations (defined by antiparallel sublattices - blue arrows) used to measure: the (a) evolution of the 𝑇௭ component with rotation of the sample normal, and the (b,c) 𝑇௬,௫ components of the XMCD vector. (d) Modulation of the XMCD signal under increasing angle of incidence, 𝜃, from the normal, at T = 300 K. (e) XMCD intensity at 𝐿ଶ-edge peak (722.25 eV) as a function of 𝜃. Figure 4 | Fe L-edge XMCD signature of the weak canted moment. (a) Experimental grazing-incidence (75°) geometry used to isolate the XMCD response of the weak moment (purple arrow), emerging from the canting of antiparallel sublattices (blue arrows). (b,c) XMCD spectra measured for c-cut crystal above 𝑇ெ (T = 300 K), under (b) positive (blue) and (c) negative (red) applied magnetic fields parallel to the X-ray beam. Dashed and solid lines correspond to field strengths of 0.1 T and 14 T, respectively. Figure 5 | Fe L-edge X-ray vectorial imaging of altermagnetic domains in thin films of α-Fe2O3. (a-d) X-PEEM imaging of the easy-plane phase (T = 400 K) and (e-h) easy-axis phase (T = 358 K). This includes: (a,e) XMLD X-PEEM images, (b,f) XMLD Néel vector maps, (c,g) XMCD-PEEM images and (d,h) the full vectorial maps, constructed as described in Methods. In (a,e), yellow and orange colours indicate orthogonal in-plane 𝑳 orientations, while purple defines out-of-plane 𝑳. In (b,f), R-G-B and black define in-plane 𝑳 orientations along the a-axes and m-axes, respectively. The angular dependence is defined by the inset colour wheel.
